coredata.dc:title: Producing long afterglow by cellulose confinement effect: A wood-inspired design for sustainable phosphorescent materials
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ping
Zheng, Dongxiao
Liu, Shouxin
Luo, Mengkai
Li, Jian
Shen, Shen
Li, Shujun
Zhu, Liangliang
Chen, Zhijun
coredata.dc:description: Embedding luminogens into a well-selected rigid matrix has been a particularly attractive way of preparing long afterglow materials. On this basis, seeking green, low-cost and sustainable materials or strategies, remains challenging but desirable. Prompted by the generation of wood fluorescence, a general strategy for preparing long afterglow materials successfully developed by embedding biomass-derived carbon dots into cellulose fibrils. Internal hydrogen bonding interactions between cellulose fibrils and carbon dots strongly erected a confinement effect for protecting the triplet exciton from quenching, leading to an increase of phosphorescence lifetime by seven orders of magnitude. These properties, which originated from such a green and convenient approach, were able to match most of reported long afterglow examples. Moreover, thanks to the humidity sensitivity of cellulose, the materials can behave a unique humidity-dependent phosphorescence property, showing superior potential application in advanced anti-counterfeiting and encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long afterglow materials
Carbon dots
Cellulose
Humidity sensors
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Long afterglow properties of the CDs@Cellulose
Sensitivity to excitation, temperature, and humidity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 31890770 ]; Special Project for Double First-Class --Cultivation of Innovative Talents , [grant number 000/41113102 ]; the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST [grant number 2018QNRC001 ].

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 31890770]; Special Project for Double First-Class –Cultivation of Innovative Talents, [grant number 000/41113102]; the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST [grant number 2018QNRC001].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Design of wood-inspired long afterglow material by embedding CDs into cellulose fibrils. Inset: Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of wood slice, scale bar = 50 μm.

(a) Schematic illustration of preparation of CDs@Cellulose; (b) TEM images of CMC CDs@Cellulose, scale bar (left) = 50 nm; scale bar (right) = 10 nm; Carbon dots were pointed out with red dotted line; (c) XRD pattern of CMC CDs@Cellulose and microcrystalline cellulose; (d) XPS survey of CMC CDs@Cellulose. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Fluorescence and phosphorescence of CMC CDs@Cellulose upon excitation at 375 nm without (red curve) and with (black curve) delay, respectively; (b) Phosphorescence lifetime of CMC CDs@Cellulose monitored at 560 nm, with 375 nm excitation; (c) Images of afterglow emission of diffrerent CDs@Cellulose after removal of 360 nm UV excitation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Phosphorescence of CMC CDs@Cellulose upon excitation with wavelengths from 355 nm to 395 nm; (b) Phosphorescence of CMC CDs@Cellulose at different temperatures; (c) Plot of phosphorescence lifetime vs humidity; (d) Phosphorescence lifetime of CMC CDs@Cellulose under relative humidity (RH): 33%; (e) RH: 57%; (f) RH: 75%; (g) RH: 94%.

(a) Photographs of QR code printed using CMC CDs@Cellulose under conditions of different relative humidity; far right, scanning the afterglow of QR code with a smart phone; (b) Fluorescent images of “QUE” printed using CMC CDs@Cellulose (highlight with red line) and fluorescent CDs (highlight with purple dotted line) upon UV excitation and removal of UV source (365 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Producing long afterglow by cellulose confinement effect: A wood-inspired design for sustainable phosphorescent materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Department of Macromolecular Science, Fudan University, Shanghai, 200438, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Department of Macromolecular Science, Fudan University, Shanghai, 200438, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Embedding luminogens into a well-selected rigid matrix has been a particularly attractive way of preparing long afterglow materials. On this basis, seeking green, low-cost and sustainable materials or strategies, remains challenging but desirable. Prompted by the generation of wood fluorescence, a general strategy for preparing long afterglow materials successfully developed by embedding biomass-derived carbon dots into cellulose fibrils. Internal hydrogen bonding interactions between cellulose fibrils and carbon dots strongly erected a confinement effect for protecting the triplet exciton from quenching, leading to an increase of phosphorescence lifetime by seven orders of magnitude. These properties, which originated from such a green and convenient approach, were able to match most of reported long afterglow examples. Moreover, thanks to the humidity sensitivity of cellulose, the materials can behave a unique humidity-dependent phosphorescence property, showing superior potential application in advanced anti-counterfeiting and encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the resolving limit of the naked eye, long afterglow phenomenon typically refers to a material emission with lifetime more than 0.1 s after the removal of the irradiation or excitation source at room temperature [1–3]. As it has huge potential in encryption [4–8], bioimaging [9–14] and sensing [11,15–17], there has been thus great interest in the design and development of long afterglow materials [18,19]. The past decade witnessed three typical new approaches for preparing long afterglow materials, i. e. 1) rigid matrix protection for multi-component afterglow materials [20], 2) H-aggregation induced afterglow in single-component organic systems [5,21], and 3) radical-ion-pair (RIP) mechanism for afterglow [22]. These approaches beneficially prevent the interaction between the triplet state and environmental oxygen, and also reduce non-radiative transition for improving the phosphorescence property [23,24]. Amongst these, the preparation of afterglow materials by confining a luminogen into a rigid matrix is more general and attractive, since such materials can be simply prepared and the emission is easily tuned. However, these rigid matrices largely rely on synthesis of fussy frameworks or the analogues [25–28]. These prepared substances are all difficult to be degraded, which can definitely be harmful to the environment over time [29,30]. Additionally, lots of these matrix materials were expensive and difficult to be prepared, which hindered their further applications. As a result, seeking green, low-cost and sustainable materials or strategies for producing long-afterglow emission, remains challenging but desirable.

Recently, the active fluorescence of lignin in wood cell walls has been attributable to a kind of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) behavior [31,32]. Restriction of the rotation of the aromatic groups of lignin within cellulose and hemicellulose in the wood cell walls is suggested to the key role of producing the strong emission [32]. Inspired by the AIE mechanism of lignin, we reasoned that it might be possible to create long afterglow materials by confining well-selected luminogens into a cellulose matrix with strong internal hydrogen bonding interactions to improve the phosphorescence property. Actually, cellulose is a linear chain of ringed d-glucose molecules linked by -(1,4) glucosidic bonds (Supplementary Fig. S1). These ringed d-glucose molecules possess abundant hydroxyl groups, which can facilitate abundant hydrogen bonding in both intra- and inter molecular chain [33,34]. Carbon dots (CDs), a luminescent material of which has sizes below 10 nm [35–46], have been applied in bioimaging [47–49], sensing [48,50], catalysis [51,52], and light-emitting diodes [50,53] due to its excellent physical chemical and optical properties. Most of CDs are sustainable and can be even synthesized from biomass materials [54–56]. The phosphorescence of CDs was also discovered recently [6,8,57–60]. Acting on this hypothesis, by optimization, we eventually developed a strategy of confining biomass-derived CDs within microcrystalline cellulose, revealing it is generally applicable for the conversion of normal fluorescent CDs into long afterglow materials.

Specifically, the design takes advantage of the following steps: 1) microcrystalline cellulose was monomerically dissolved in a solution of NaOH and urea; 2) An aqueous solution of CDs was then added to the sufficiently dispersed microcrystalline cellulose, causing a successive aggregation of the cellulose molecular chains with a reformation of cellulose fibrils in which the CDs were spontaneously embedded. In this case, strong hydrogen bonding interactions can be well characterized between the cellulose molecular chains and surficial carbonyl moieties on the CDs, allowing the extension of its afterglow into 167.31 ms. Such a new long afterglow system, termed CDs@Cellulose, can be easily prepared from cheap and sustainable materials, and may exert great potential for large-scale preparation (see the outline in Fig. 1 ).

Carboxymethyl cellulose, sodium alginate, citric acid, NaOH and urea were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Microcrystalline cellulose was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Solvents were purchased from Aladdin or Tokyo Chemical Industry (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was produced using a Smart-RO ultrapure water system (Hitech Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

Fluorescence was measured using a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Edison, NJ, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted using a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed using ESCALAB 250x X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (American Thermo Co., Ltd., Waltham, Massachusetts) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a TU-1901 UV–Vis double-beam spectrophotometer (Persee General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). XRD pattern were recorded using an X’Pert3 powder X-ray powder diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Netherlands). The temperature-dependent afterglow spectra、excitation spectrum at different temperature and lifetime decay curves of the samples were recorded using an FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Livingston, UK), equipped with a xenon lamp and a microsecond flashlamp (detector: photomultiplier tube, 200 nm
< λ <
1700 nm). Temperature was controlled using a OX135QX cryostat (Oxford Instrument plc, Tubney Woods, Abingdon, UK) and afterglow emission spectra were measured after a delay of 5 ms. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted on a Mettler Toledo TGA/SDTA/851e analyser from 25 to 600 °C with heating at 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere.

Preparation of CMC CDs: A solution of sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (4320 mg) and ethylenediamine (2 mL) in deionized water (200 mL) was placed in a thick-walled reaction bottle and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The reaction vessel was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature and the solution was freeze-dried to provide CMC CDs.

Preparation of CA CDs: CA CDs were prepared as previously described [49]. A solution of citric acid (1.0507 g) and ethylenediamine (335 μL) in deionized water (10 mL) was heated at 150 °C for 5 h in a 30 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. The reactor was cooled to room temperature, by water or naturally, and the brown-black transparent solution was dialyzed and freeze-dried to provide the CDs.

Preparation of SA CDs: The SA CDs were prepared as previously described [56]. A solution of sodium alginate (100 g) and ethylenediamine (12 mL) in ultrapure water (5 L) was transferred to a reaction kettle and stirred at 180 °C for 8 h. The kettle was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature and the solution was freeze-dried to provide solid SA CDs.

Preparation of three types of CDs@Cellulose: CDs (CMC CDs, CA CDs or SA CDs; 0.5 g) were mixed in a beaker with a solution of microcrystalline cellulose (0.2 g) in NaOH/urea/water (7:12:81; 20 mL). The mixture was frozen at −12 °C overnight, allowed to thaw to room temperature, and ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min. Deionized water (100 mL) was then added with stirring and the resulting mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 11 min. The precipitate was washed by deionized water for three times. As-obtained precipitate was collected as precipitate and the CDs@Cellulose were obtained by freeze-drying.

Measurement of phosphorescence lifetime of CMC CDs@Cellulose under different relative humidity: Environments with different relative humidity were prepared using saturated solutions of salts with known relative humidity. The chosen salts, MgCl2·H2O, NaBr, NaCl and KNO3, had relative humidity of 33%, 57%, 75% and 94%, respectively, at 25 °C. The saturated solutions were prepared at 40 °C and allowed to equilibrate at room temperature for 24 h. The phosphorescence lifetimes of the CMC CDs@Cellulose were then investigated via in situ monitoring in air with different relative humidity.

Firstly, sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and ethylenediamine were reacted using a hydrothermal method to prepare CMC CDs. The as-obtained CMC CDs have an average particle diameter of ∼1.97 nm (see Supplementary Fig. S2 and Table S1). The CMC CDs showed excitation-dependent emission upon irradiation with 300–400 nm UV light, which is very typical in the emission behavior of the CD family caused by surface functional groups, also known as molecular states (like CO) [61]. The fluorescence lifetime of CMC CDs was 7.11 ns (Supplementary Fig. S3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed that the CMC CDs contained the elements C (∼57%), N (∼10%), O (∼28%) and Na (∼5%) (Supplementary Fig. S4). High resolution scans on C、N and O showed CMC CDs contained C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and CO moieties on the surface. (Supplementary Fig. S4), which can be well connected to the phosphorescence property (vide infra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long afterglow properties of the CDs@Cellulose
ce:section.ce:para: Having determined the morphology and structural properties of CMC CDs@Cellulose, we next investigated the room-temperature phosphorescence emission. The fluorescence emission and afterglow emission peaks of CMC CDs@Cellulose were located at 460 nm and 560 nm, respectively, under excitation at 375 nm without and with delay, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The difference between the emissions without and with delay demonstrates that the latter one is the result of the afterglow. The lifetime of the phosphorescence band was 167.31 ms at room temperature (τ1 = 4.19 ms (11.02%), τ2 = 27.76 ms (33.6%) and τ3 = 181.04 ms (55.38%)), a typical long afterglow characteristic (Eq. S1 and Table S2). As a comparison, fluorescence lifetime of CMC CDs@Cellulose was also studied and the value was 6.55 ns Interestingly, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs@Cellulose decreased from 6.55 ns to 3.1 ns when the temperature went up from room temperature to 150 °C. This was attributed to the promoted non-radiative migration of CDs@Cellulose caused by heating (Supplementary Fig. S6) [64]. The CMC CDs have two absorption bands, centered at 265 and 324 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π∗ transition of CC and the n-π∗ transition of CO, respectively. The overlap of the afterglow excitation spectrum and the absorption spectrum over the range 300–400 nm shows that phosphorescence originates mainly from the CO bond of the CMC CDs (Supplementary Fig. S7), in agreement with the fact that carbonyl can help enhance spin-orbital coupling (SOC) for phosphorescence emission [65].

In addition to the CO bond of the CMC CDs that can facilitate the production of the phosphorescence, we denote that the cellulose matrix is quite crucial for prolonging the phosphorescence lifetime. In the experiments and analysis described above, we have confirmed that the CMC CDs are embedded in the matrix of cellulose fibrils. Therefore, the strong hydrogen bonds within the cellulose captured the triplet species (that is, CO containing moieties) on the CMC CDs and protected the triplet excitons from quenching, thus to promote the phosphorescence property. Specifically, the ʋ(O–H) and ʋ(CO) of CMC CDs@Cellulose exhibited a remarkable shift in FTIR spectra, relative to the precursors, indicating a strong hydrogen bond between CDs and Cellulose formed upon weakening their original non-covalent interactions (Supplementary Fig. S8). Additionally, the shoulder peak at 3270 cm−1 of FTIR was assigned as the intermolecular hydrogen bonds between β-1,4-glucan chains and CDs [66]. To further verify this hypothesis, CMC CDs@Cellulose was exposed to water vapor, which can break the hydrogen bond interactions between the CDs and cellulose fibrils. This exposure significantly quenched the long afterglow (Supplementary Fig. S9), suggesting that the intermolecular hydrogen bonding is crucial for the unique phosphorescence property.

To demonstrate the generality of this method, more CDs were synthesized from sodium alginate (SA CDs) and citric acid (CA CDs). XPS analysis was also conducted to determine their structures (Supplementary Figs. S10 and S11). Fluorescence emission and lifetime of these carbon dots were also recorded (SA CDs, 5.47 ns; CA CDs, 13 ns) (Supplementary Figs. S12 and S13). After that, these CDs were also embedded into cellulose fibrils using the same solvent exchange method as for CMC CDs@Cellulose. Gratifyingly, these two materials emitted long-lifetime phosphorescence upon UV excitation (Fig. 3c). In a control experiment, none of the CDs themselves showed phosphorescence without the cellulose matrix (Supplementary Fig. S14). When excited at 360 nm, the phosphorescence lifetimes of CA CDs@Cellulose and SA CDs@Cellulose were 190 ms (monitored at 530 nm) and 165 ms (monitored at 535 nm), respectively (Supplementary Fig. S15). These results also suggested the CO containing moieties in CDs embedded in the matrix of cellulose fibrils can generally exert long afterglow in this way. Interestingly, the phosphorescence of CA CDs@Cellulose showed shorter emission wavelength than the other two materials, probably due to the sodium ions in CMC CDs and SA CDs, which affected their corresponding surface states and energy levels in CDs [67,68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity to excitation, temperature, and humidity
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different excitation wavelength, temperature and humidity on the phosphorescence of CMC CDs@Cellulose were investigated next. Similar to the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, CMC CDs@Cellulose showed interesting excitation-dependent phosphorescence. The afterglow emission of CMC CDs@Cellulose peaked largely at 540 nm and gradually red-shifted to 560 nm with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 4 a). A same trend was also observed at low temperature (77 K), featuring the intrinsic attributes of the long afterglow materials (Supplementary Fig. S16). The excitation-dependent behavior of the afterglow was attributed to the existence of multiple triplet-excited states in CMC CDs@Cellulose, and this was confirmed by the fact that the lifetime of the afterglow decay curve of CMC CDs@Cellulose at 375 nm excitation can be fitted to a 3-exponential function (Fig. 3b), calculated by equation S1 (see Supporting information for details) [8,60,69].

The intensity of the afterglow emission decreased when the temperature was increased from 100 K to 350 K (Fig. 4b), in keeping with the expected reduction in phosphorescence at elevated temperatures because of increased nonradiative decay. Excitation spectra of CMC CDs@Cellulose also showed a similar trend, but without wavelength shift. This further suggested the afterglow emission came from CO, consistent with the previous discussion (Supplementary Fig. S17). Stronger phosphorescence emission and longer lifetime of CMC CDs@Cellulose pre-sintered at high temperature were observed (Supplementary Fig. S18). This might be contributed to the higher structural regularity and the crystallinity of cellulose caused by high temperature [63]. As discussed above, the phosphorescence of CMC CDs@Cellulose is easily quenched by water vapor since water can break the hydrogen bonding interactions between the CDs and cellulose fibrils. Here, the effect of relative humidity (RH) on the phosphorescence was investigated quantitatively (Fig. 4d–g). Exposure of CMC CDs@Cellulose to increasing RH led to decreased lifetime of emission (namely, 110.72 ms, 91.7 ms, 78.46 ms and 25.75 ms at RH = 33%, 57%, 75% and 94%, respectively). Interestingly, the decrease in the lifetime of the phosphorescence showed a trend towards linearity over a moderate RH variation range (Fig. 4c).

The humidity-responsive long afterglow of the CMC CDs@Cellulose suggests application as an indicator of environmental humidity, the control of which is very important for advanced anti-counterfeiting. To demonstrate the feasibility of this potential application, a QR code was printed onto a label using CMC CDs@Cellulose under conditions of ∼50% humidity or below (Fig. 5 a). The long afterglow emission of the QR code could be scanned by a smart phone after removal of UV irradiation (Fig. 5a and Video S1).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.09.060

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Video S1
2
Video S1

When the humidity was increased to 75% or more, however, the long afterglow emission essentially disappeared after removal of the excitation source and the QR code became unavailable (Fig. 5a). These results indicate that CMC CDs@Cellulose can be used as a humidity indicator to monitor the information authenticity. The potential of CMC CDs@Cellulose for use in encryption was also evaluated. The three characters “QUE” were printed using CMC CDs@Cellulose and fluorescent CDs, together. As shown in Fig. 5b, a fluorescent image of “QUE”, produced by these CDs, was observed upon excitation with UV light. The fluorescence part (highlight with purple dotted line) disappeared immediately after removal of the excitation source and long afterglow emission of the CMC CDs@Cellulose (highlight with red line) maintained for a while. As a result, the number “O111” could be observed after turning off the excitation source (Fig. 5b).

Inspired by the fluorescence of lignin, we have developed a general strategy for preparing green and sustainable long afterglow materials by confining CDs into biomass-derived cellulose fibrils. The as-prepared long afterglow materials demonstrated unique long afterglow phosphorescence properties with excitation/temperature/humidity dependent. Especially because of their sensitivity to the change in humidity, the long afterglow materials have the potential to be used to monitor storage conditions for moisture-sensitive materials, revealing superior potential application in advanced anti-counterfeiting and encryption. These results show generality of employing cellulose fibrils to improve material luminescent properties, which could be valuable for enlightening the development of future low-cost and sustainable materials for high-tech applications in a large scale. In the future, such an as-developed sustainable cellulose confinement strategy might allow a preparation of more advanced long afterglow phosphorescence materials together with organic chromophores.

Ping Wang: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Dongxiao Zheng: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Shouxin Liu: Formal analysis. Mengkai Luo: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jian Li: Formal analysis, Supervision. Shen Shen: Investigation, Formal analysis. Shujun Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Liangliang Zhu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis. Zhijun Chen: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 31890770]; Special Project for Double First-Class --Cultivation of Innovative Talents, [grant number 000/41113102]; the Young Elite Scientists Sponsorship Program by CAST [grant number 2018QNRC001].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.09.060.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient organic afterglow from a 2D layered lead-free metal halide in both crystals and thin films under an air atmosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yang, B. Zhou, Q. Huang, S. Wang, H. Zhen, D. Yan, et al., Highly Efficient Organic Afterglow from a 2D Layered Lead-Free Metal Halide in Both Crystals and Thin Films under an Air Atmosphere, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces (2019) https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.9b20502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excited state modulation for organic afterglow: materials and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Xu, R. Chen, C. Zheng, W. Huang, Excited State Modulation for Organic Afterglow: Materials and Applications, Adv. Mater. 28 (2016) 9920-9940.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Resonance-activated spin-flipping for efficient organic ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Tao, R. Chen, H. Li, J. Yuan, Y. Wan, H. Jiang, et al., Resonance-Activated Spin-Flipping for Efficient Organic Ultralong Room-Temperature Phosphorescence, Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1803856.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design efficient and ultralong pure organic room-temperature phosphorescent materials by structural isomerism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xiong, Z. Zhao, W. Zhao, H. Ma, Q. Peng, Z. He, et al., Design Efficient and Ultralong Pure Organic Room-Temperature Phosphorescent Materials by Structural Isomerism, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 57 (2018) 7997-8001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stabilizing triplet excited states for ultralong organic phosphorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. An, C. Zheng, Y. Tao, R. Chen, H. Shi, T. Chen, et al., Stabilizing Triplet Excited States for Ultralong Organic Phosphorescence, Nat. Mater. 14 (2015) 685.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conversion of carbon dots from fluorescence to ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence by heating for security applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, Y. Wang, C. Cai, H. Lin, Conversion of Carbon Dots from Fluorescence to Ultralong Room-Temperature Phosphorescence by Heating for Security Applications, Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1800783.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation-dependent long-life luminescent polymeric systems under ambient conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Su, Y. Zhang, Z. Wang, W. Gao, P. Jia, D. Zhang, et al., Excitation-Dependent Long-Life Luminescent Polymeric Systems under Ambient Conditions, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. (2019) https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201912102.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triple-mode emission of carbon dots: applications for advanced anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, L. Zhang, J. Lu, C. Xu, C. Cai, H. Lin, Triple-Mode Emission of Carbon Dots: Applications for Advanced Anti-Counterfeiting, Angew. Chem. Int Ed., 55 (2016) 7231-7235.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong phosphorescence of water-soluble organic nanoparticles for in vivo afterglow imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhen, Y. Tao, Z. An, P. Chen, C. Xu, R. Chen, et al., Ultralong Phosphorescence of Water-Soluble Organic Nanoparticles for In Vivo Afterglow Imaging, Adv. Mater. 29 (2017) 1606665.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional near infrared-emitting Cr3+/Pr3+ Co-doped zinc gallogermanate persistent luminescent nanoparticles with superlong afterglow for in vivo targeted bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Abdukayum, J. Chen, Q. Zhao, X. Yan, Functional Near Infrared-Emitting Cr3+/Pr3+ Co-Doped Zinc Gallogermanate Persistent Luminescent Nanoparticles with Superlong Afterglow for in Vivo Targeted Bioimaging, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 135 (2013) 14125-14133.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-phosphorescent iridium (III) complexes extending oxygen sensing from hypoxia to hyperoxia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Abdukayum, J. Chen, Q. Zhao, X. Yan, Dual-Phosphorescent Iridium (III) Complexes Extending Oxygen Sensing from Hypoxia to Hyperoxia, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 140 (2018) 7827−7834.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pure organic room temperature phosphorescence from excited dimers in self-assembled nanoparticles under visible and nearinfrared irradiation in water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, H. Xiao, P. Chen, Q. Yang, B. Chen, C. Tung, et al., Pure Organic Room Temperature Phosphorescence from Excited Dimers in Self-Assembled Nanoparticles under Visible and NearInfrared Irradiation in Water, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 141 (2019) 5045−5050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared afterglow luminescent aggregation-induced emission dots with ultrahigh tumor-to-liver signal ratio for promoted image-guided cancer surgery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Ni, X. Zhang, X. Duan, H. Zheng, X. Xue, D. Ding, Near-Infrared Afterglow Luminescent Aggregation-Induced Emission Dots with Ultrahigh Tumor-to-Liver Signal Ratio for Promoted Image-Guided Cancer Surgery, Nano Lett. 19 (2019) 318−330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembled semiconducting polymer nanoparticles for ultrasensitive near-infrared afterglow imaging of metastatic tumors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Xie, X. Zhen, Q. Miao, Y. Lyu, K. Pu, Self-Assembled Semiconducting Polymer Nanoparticles for Ultrasensitive Near-Infrared Afterglow Imaging of Metastatic Tumors, Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1801331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alkaline-earth and aminonicotinate based coordination polymers with combined fluorescence/long-lasting phosphorescence and metal ion sensing response

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. C. Oier, R. D. Antonio, B. Garikoitz, P. Y. Sonia, A. G. Jose, S. S.
